title: Ad-Click / Analytics Pipeline — System Design
description: Design a pipeline that ingests billions of ad impression and click events per day and turns them into accurate, queryable campaign metrics. The themes are high-throughput ingestion, stream vs batch processing, deduplication for correct counts, attribution, and the freshness-vs-accuracy tradeoff.

nodes.label: Ad Client / Browser
nodes.description: Emits impression and click events as users interact with ads.

nodes.label: Event Collector (Edge)
nodes.description: Lightweight endpoint that validates, enriches, and accepts events fast.

nodes.label: Event Log (Kafka)
nodes.description: Durable, partitioned, ordered event log buffering all ingested events.

nodes.label: Stream Processor
nodes.description: Real-time consumer: dedup, windowed aggregation, attribution.

nodes.label: Batch Processor
nodes.description: Periodic reprocessing of raw events for accurate, corrected metrics.

nodes.label: Raw Event Store (Data Lake)
nodes.description: Immutable archive of every raw event for replay and batch recompute.

nodes.label: Dedup Store
nodes.description: Seen-event-id set / Bloom filter for exactly-once-ish counting.

nodes.label: Query / Dashboard API
nodes.description: Serves aggregated metrics to dashboards and advertisers.

nodes.label: Dashboard / Advertiser
nodes.description: Views near-real-time and historical campaign metrics.

nodes.label: Attribution Service
nodes.description: Joins clicks to conversions within a window to credit campaigns.

functional: Ingest impression and click events at very high throughput.
Produce near-real-time metrics (clicks, CTR) within seconds.
Produce accurate, corrected daily metrics (the billing source of truth).
Attribute conversions to the click/campaign that drove them.
Let advertisers query metrics sliced by campaign, time, geo, etc.
nonFunctional: Durable ingestion: never lose an accepted event (billing depends on it).
Correct counts: dedup retried/duplicate events (no over-counting).
Low query latency on aggregated metrics.
Reprocessable: fix a bug and recompute history from raw events.

capacity.label: Events per second
capacity.note: Impressions dominate; clicks are a fraction.

api.description: Ingest a batch of ad events. Accepted fast and durably; processed downstream.
Query aggregated metrics for a campaign over a time range.

dataModel.name: raw_event
dataModel.note: Immutable record of one ad event (in the log and data lake).

dataModel.name: metric_rollup
dataModel.note: Pre-aggregated metrics in the OLAP store.

patterns.name: Durable event log (Kafka)
patterns.what: Append events to a partitioned, replayable log that decouples ingestion from processing.
patterns.whenToUse: High-throughput ingestion where producers and consumers scale independently.

patterns.name: Stream processing (speed layer)
patterns.what: Consume the log continuously to produce low-latency, approximate metrics.

patterns.name: Batch reprocessing (batch layer)
patterns.what: Recompute metrics from immutable raw events for accuracy and bug fixes.
patterns.whenToUse: When billing-grade correctness and replayability are required.

patterns.name: Idempotent dedup
patterns.what: Use an event id + a seen-set/Bloom filter so retries don't over-count.
patterns.whenToUse: At-least-once delivery where counts must be correct.

patterns.name: Windowed attribution
patterns.what: Join clicks to later conversions within a time window to credit campaigns.
patterns.whenToUse: Conversion/ROI metrics that span multiple events.

patterns.name: Pre-aggregation / rollups
patterns.what: Materialize metrics by time bucket and dimension so queries are cheap.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Increment a Counter Row Per Event (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. When an event arrives, the collector synchronously does an UPDATE that increments a counter row in the metrics store (clicks++ for this campaign), and dashboards read those rows directly.

This is correct at low volume and exposes three problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, a synchronous increment per event can't keep up with ~1M events/sec — the hot counter rows become a write bottleneck and ingestion stalls or drops events the moment processing falls behind. Second, ingestion is at-least-once: a retried or double-fired event increments the counter twice, so billed clicks are over-counted with no way to tell. Third, once a number is added into a counter the raw event is gone, so a counting bug can never be reprocessed out of history.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: accept events into a durable, replayable log first, count asynchronously with idempotent dedup, and keep the raw events so a batch layer can recompute accurate, billing-grade numbers.
steps.decisionPrompt: If each event is a synchronous counter increment, what happens at 1M events/sec, and how do you bill correctly when an event is delivered twice?
whyNow: Establishing the synchronous-counter baseline first makes it obvious why ingestion must be decoupled and durable, why counting needs dedup, and why raw events must be kept — every later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: Each event synchronously increments a counter row that dashboards read directly.
recap.newRisk: Synchronous counting can't absorb the firehose, retries over-count billed events, and a counting bug can't be reprocessed.

traps.trap: Counting events with a synchronous per-event counter increment.
traps.why: It bottlenecks at the firehose, double-counts at-least-once retries, and discards the raw events needed to fix history.
traps.instead: Append events to a durable log, count asynchronously with idempotent dedup, and keep raw events for batch recompute.

steps.title: 2. Durable High-Throughput Ingestion
description: A lightweight edge collector validates and enriches each event and appends it to a durable, partitioned event log (Kafka-style). The collector does almost no work so it can absorb ~1M events/sec.

The log decouples producers from consumers: ingestion never blocks on downstream processing, and many consumers can read independently at their own pace. Partition by a key (e.g. ad/campaign id) for parallelism and ordering within a partition.
Also tee the raw events into an immutable data lake — the system of record that makes later reprocessing possible.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why put a log between the collector and the processors instead of processing events inline?

concepts.definition: An append-only, partitioned, replicated, replayable stream of events.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decouples ingestion from processing and enables replay — the backbone of the pipeline.
concepts.example: Kafka topic partitioned by ad_id, retained for days, consumed by stream + archived to a lake.

whyNow: The log is the architectural backbone; everything (real-time, batch, replay) hangs off durable, decoupled ingestion.

recap.after: Events durably accepted and buffered, decoupled from processing.
recap.newRisk: Raw events aren't metrics yet — need processing, and counts must handle duplicates.

traps.trap: Doing aggregation synchronously in the collector.
traps.why: Ties ingestion throughput/availability to processing; a slow consumer drops events.
traps.instead: Accept fast to a durable log; process asynchronously.

steps.title: 3. Speed Layer: Real-Time Metrics
description: A stream processor consumes the log continuously and maintains windowed aggregates (clicks/impressions per campaign per minute), writing them to the OLAP store within seconds.
This is the speed layer: fast and fresh, but approximate — it may miss late-arriving events or contain duplicates not yet reconciled.
Use tumbling/sliding time windows keyed by campaign+dimensions; emit incremental updates so dashboards move in near-real-time.
steps.decisionPrompt: What does the speed layer get wrong that you'll need to correct later?

concepts.definition: Grouping streaming events into time windows to compute running metrics.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns an unbounded stream into bounded, queryable rollups.
concepts.example: Per-minute click counts per campaign, updated as events arrive.

whyNow: Real-time freshness is a hard requirement; the stream layer delivers it, while explicitly being the approximate half of the design.

recap.newRisk: Retries/duplicates over-count; late events are missed — correctness needs more.

steps.title: 4. Deduplicate for Correct Counts
description: Ingestion is at-least-once: network retries and client re-fires mean the same event can appear multiple times. Counting naively over-counts clicks — and clicks are billed.

Give each event a stable event id. The stream processor checks a dedup store (a seen-id set, often a Bloom filter or RocksDB-backed keyed state) and ignores ids it has already counted — effectively-once counting.
Bound the dedup window (you can't remember every id forever); the batch layer provides the exact, full-history dedup as the backstop.
steps.decisionPrompt: A click event is delivered twice. How do you make sure the advertiser is billed once?

concepts.definition: Skip events whose id has already been processed.
concepts.whyItMatters: Converts at-least-once delivery into correct, exactly-once counts.
concepts.example: Bloom filter of seen event ids per window; a repeat id is dropped.

whyNow: Correct counts are non-negotiable for billing; dedup is the mechanism and the at-least-once reality is the reason.

recap.newRisk: Approximate dedup over a window isn't perfect; conversions span events — need attribution and an exact backstop.

traps.trap: Assuming the message bus gives exactly-once for free.
traps.why: Most pipelines are at-least-once at the edges; producers retry.
traps.instead: Make counting idempotent on a stable event id; reconcile exactly in batch.

flows.name: Effectively-once counting via dedup
flows.note: The stream processor checks the dedup store by event id; a first-seen event is counted, a duplicate is dropped so billed clicks aren't inflated.

steps.title: 5. Attribution: Credit Clicks for Conversions
description: Advertisers care about conversions (a purchase), which happen later than and separately from the click. Attribution joins a conversion back to the click/campaign that drove it, within an attribution window (e.g. 7 days).

This is a stateful, time-windowed join across event types and time — heavier than counting. The stream layer can do near-real-time attribution for recent windows; longer windows and corrections are done in batch over raw events.
Define the model explicitly (last-click, first-click, multi-touch) — it changes who gets credit and the numbers advertisers see.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user clicks an ad Monday and buys Thursday. How do you credit the campaign, and what's the state cost?

concepts.definition: Joining a conversion to a prior click within a bounded time window.
concepts.whyItMatters: Produces ROI/conversion metrics that span multiple events and days.
concepts.example: Last-click within 7 days credits the most recent click's campaign.

whyNow: Attribution is the ad-specific depth that separates this from a generic counter; the windowed join and model choice are the senior signals.

recap.after: Conversions attributed to campaigns within a window.
recap.newRisk: Long windows + corrections exceed what the speed layer can hold accurately — batch must reconcile.

steps.title: 6. Batch Layer: Accurate, Reprocessable Metrics
description: Periodically reprocess the immutable raw events end-to-end to produce billing-grade metrics: full dedup, complete attribution windows, and late-arriving events all included.
Batch results overwrite the speed layer's approximate numbers for the same windows (lambda-architecture reconciliation): real-time numbers are provisional, batch numbers are authoritative.
Because the raw events are immutable and replayable, a processing bug is fixed by re-running batch over history — no data loss, just recompute.
steps.decisionPrompt: You discover the click counter double-counted a class of events for a week. How do you fix history?

concepts.definition: Pair a fast approximate stream layer with an accurate batch (or replay) layer that corrects it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Delivers both freshness and correctness, and makes bugs recoverable.
concepts.example: Batch overwrites today's hourly rollups once the day is complete.

whyNow: The accuracy + reprocessability requirements demand a batch/replay layer; framing speed-vs-batch is the core architectural tradeoff of the case.

recap.after: Authoritative batch metrics; history is recomputable.
recap.newRisk: Two sources for the same metric — queries must know which to trust; serving must hide the seam.

failureDrills.scenario: The stream processor crashes and loses in-memory windows.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It resumes from the log offset (checkpointed state); batch later corrects any gap.
failureDrills.mitigation: Replayable log + checkpointed stream state + authoritative batch recompute.

flows.name: Batch recompute overwrites provisional metrics
flows.note: Batch replays immutable raw events for a closed window with full dedup and attribution, then overwrites the speed layer's approximate numbers.

messages.label: replay raw events for closed day

steps.title: 7. Serve: Pre-Aggregation and Query
description: Dashboards can't scan billions of raw events per query. Store pre-aggregated rollups by time bucket and dimension in an OLAP/columnar store; queries read materialized aggregates and are fast.

The query API merges the two sources cleanly: serve batch (authoritative) numbers for closed windows and speed-layer (provisional) numbers for the current, still-open window — so users see fresh data that later settles to exact.
Roll up at multiple granularities (minute -> hour -> day) and pre-compute common dimension slices to keep queries cheap.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does the query API give a user fresh numbers now and exact numbers later without contradicting itself?

concepts.definition: Materializing metrics by window+dimension ahead of query time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns an O(events) scan into an O(buckets) lookup.
concepts.example: Per-hour campaign rollups answer a month-long query from ~720 rows.

whyNow: Serving ties it together: it's where the freshness/accuracy split becomes a single coherent answer for the user.

recap.before: Metrics computed but not efficiently queryable.
recap.after: Fast dashboards over rollups; provisional-then-exact merge.
recap.newRisk: Rollup cardinality explosion across many dimensions — bounded by limiting pre-computed slices.

bottlenecks.issue: High-cardinality dimension combinations blow up rollup storage.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Pre-compute only common slices; compute rare ones on demand from raw.

bottlenecks.issue: A hot partition (huge campaign) lags the stream.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Repartition / scale consumers; the log absorbs the backlog.

flows.name: Provisional-then-exact query merge
flows.note: The query API serves authoritative batch numbers for closed windows and provisional speed-layer numbers for the still-open window, hiding the seam.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Lambda Analytics Pipeline
finalDesign.description: A thin collector accepts events fast into a durable partitioned log, which is both consumed by a stream processor (dedup via event-id seen-set, windowed aggregation and near-real-time attribution -> OLAP) and archived raw to a data lake. A batch layer periodically reprocesses the immutable raw events for exact, fully-attributed, billing-grade metrics that overwrite the provisional speed-layer rollups. A query API serves pre-aggregated rollups, merging authoritative batch numbers for closed windows with provisional stream numbers for the open window. Replayable raw events make any bug recoverable by recompute.

functional.how: Batch reprocess of raw events overwrites provisional numbers.
Pre-aggregated OLAP rollups via query API.

nonFunctional.how: Durable replicated log + immutable data lake.
Idempotent dedup in stream + exact dedup in batch.
Pre-aggregation; queries read rollups not raw.
Immutable raw events replayed through batch.

say: Confirm both real-time freshness AND billing-grade accuracy are required.
Pin ~1M events/sec, durability, and correct (dedup'd) counts.
Establish attribution and reprocessability as first-class.
Thin collector into a durable partitioned log; archive raw.
Stream layer for real-time windowed metrics.
Dedup with event ids for correct counts.
Windowed attribution and the model choice.
Lambda reconciliation; pre-aggregation and the provisional-then-exact serving merge.
Tradeoffs: speed vs accuracy, dedup window size, rollup cardinality.
Mention kappa (replay-only) as a simpler alternative to lambda.

expectations: Ingests to a log and aggregates.
May miss dedup, attribution, and accuracy correction.
Reasons about exactly-once vs at-least-once precisely.
Discusses rollup cardinality, hot partitions, and serving-seam consistency.

followUps: Lambda vs kappa (replay-only) architecture — when would you drop the batch layer?
How do you handle very late-arriving events (days late)?
How would you support exactly-once end to end, and what does it cost?
How do you bound rollup storage as advertisers slice by many dimensions?
How would you detect and handle click fraud in this pipeline?
